one_line_takeaway: A literature-synthesis framework paper that consolidates the RAG security landscape into an 11-risk / 13-mitigation matrix and a layered governance-plus-security implementation framework, offering practitioners a structured checklist rather than any new attack, defense, or empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The RAG attack surface can be organized into 11 representative risks mapped to 13 mitigations, but no single mitigation is sufficient and several controls trade off against utility. The authors highlight that embeddings deserve the same protection level as raw data because inversion can reconstruct originals with high accuracy (per cited work), that indirect jailbreak via poisoned retrieved documents materially raises attack success rates over direct jailbreaks, that adding noise does not reliably improve RAG protection (though it can aid accuracy), and that re-ranking alone is insufficient for confidentiality. The overarching thesis is that securing RAG is not an isolated technical task but requires a holistic approach spanning IT baseline, AI/LLM, and RAG-specific layers plus organizational governance, with risk assessment driven by deployment scope, hosting model, and data-source trust.
core_contribution: A conceptual, non-empirical contribution with two parts. (1) A consolidated risk-and-mitigation matrix (Table I) for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines, synthesized from a literature review that deliberately includes non-peer-reviewed sources (preprints, blog posts, industry reports). It enumerates 11 RAG-specific risks (R0-R10) grouped into General, Vectorization/Retrieval, Operational, and Data-Manipulation categories, and 13 mitigations (M0-M12) grouped into Secure Retrieval, System Hardening, Disclosure Prevention, and Other, then cross-maps which mitigations address which risks. Risks are also anchored to a reference RAG architecture (Fig. 1: General Pipeline, Data Ingestion, Retriever, Generator) so each risk points at a concrete component. (2) A holistic, layered implementation framework (Fig. 2) that embeds the RAG-specific matrix inside three overarching activities (ML-Ops, Data Governance, Project/Risk Management) and three security layers (IT Baseline Protection, AI & LLM Protection, RAG Protection), tied to external standards (NIST RMF/CSF/AI-RMF, BSI IT-Grundschutz, ISO/IEC 27001, OWASP LLM/ML Top 10, Zero Trust Architecture, GDPR, EU AI Act). The paper's own novelty is the structuring/synthesis and the framework, NOT any of the individual attacks or defenses, which are all drawn from cited prior work.
threat_model: Adversaries targeting RAG systems that integrate sensitive or proprietary data. The attack surface is decomposed across pipeline components. Confidentiality threats: retrieval data leakage (R2, targeted vs. opportunistic), embedding inversion to reconstruct source text from vectors (R3), membership inference at sample- or user-level (R4), data disclosure during embedding (R5, worse with external embedding providers) and during prompting (R6, crafted prompts targeting specific documents), and prompt disclosure to cloud-hosted LLMs (R7). Integrity/availability threats: knowledge corruption / data poisoning by injecting or altering documents to skew retrieval ranking (R8), indirect prompt injection via malicious instructions embedded in retrieved documents (R9, especially dangerous when the generator can call APIs/tools), and indirect jailbreak via poisoned retrieved content (R10). Plus systemic risks: unknown/zero-day and blind-spot risks (R0) and inherent system limitations in handling complex data (R1). The paper stresses that risk severity is contextual: it depends on deployment scope (private vs. internal vs. public-facing), hosting (on-prem removes R6/R7 disclosure risks; cloud/hybrid adds them), and data provenance (scraped/untrusted-user data raises R8/R9; trusted-admin-only data removes R8).
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) the risk list is explicitly non-exhaustive and representative rather than complete; (2) reliance on non-peer-reviewed sources reduces the technical rigor/depth of some findings; (3) the paper does NOT evaluate the effectiveness of any mitigation and does NOT quantify how mitigations impact system performance or accuracy; (4) monitoring/validation solutions are noted as ad-hoc with no scientifically validated options available. Reviewer-added: the mitigation-to-risk cross-mapping (Table I) is asserted from literature, not validated empirically, and no cost/benefit or residual-risk quantification is provided. This is a synthesis/framework paper, so its value is as a structured checklist, not as measured evidence.
